front: Novel: title refers to a type of candy; metaphor for human nature ('bite it all the way down')

Novel: protagonist records 'God damn you...why can't you go back home forever and let me be' on a gramophone record
Novel: Kolley Kibber newspaper contest; Fred Hale drops cards for readers to find
Novel: ends with a priest declaring 'the appalling strangeness of the mercy of God'
Novel: Pinkie quotes Camden's 'Betwixt the stirrup and the ground, mercy I asked, mercy I found'
Novel: gang members Spicer, Dallow, and Cubit; Pinkie kills Spicer by pushing him down stairs
Novel: Ida conducts a seance with Old Crow; Pinkie negotiates with rival Colleoni
Novel: lawyer Prewitt quotes Marlowe's Doctor Faustus: 'this is Hell, nor are we out of it'
Novel: opens with Fred Hale trying to escape among the Whitsun crowd
Novel: private detective Parkis hired to follow Sarah; recovers her diary; discovers she has been meeting Richard Smythe
back: The End of the Affair (Graham Greene)

front: Novel: opens with 'A story has no beginning or end'; narrator calls it 'a record of hate far more than of love'
back: The End of the Affair (Graham Greene)

back: The End of the Affair (Graham Greene)

front: Novel: plays checkers with Captain Segura using whiskey bottles as pieces; steals Segura's gun
Novel: daughter Milly set classmate Thomas Earl Parkman Jr. on fire after he pulled her hair
Novel: dachshund Max drinks poisoned whiskey at a European Traders Association banquet and dies
Novel: Captain Segura owns a cigarette case made of human skin; speaks of a 'torturable class'
Novel: Hawthorne recruits protagonist; secretary Beatrice Severn; Lamb's Tales from Shakespeare used as book code
Novel: Dr. Hasselbacher killed after decoding messages; protagonist kills Carter in revenge
Novel: nude dancer Teresa rescued from the Shanghai Theater; pilot Raul killed in a car crash
Novel: protagonist tells his child people should never 'learn from experience,' citing a clown who falls into a bucket of whitewash
Novel: protagonist walks in on Hasselbacher wearing his Uhlan uniform
Short Story: Old Misery locked in an outhouse; a mattress full of money is burned
Short Story: Francis Morton dies of fright during hide-and-seek when his twin brother touches him
Short Story: from collection Twenty-One Stories; adapted as film The Fallen Idol by Carol Reed
Short Story: aspiring novelist ignores the title figures who speak an 'incomprehensible tongue'
Short Story: baker offers a boy an electric train if he steals communion wafers
Short Story: narrator converses with a famous novelist after both decline the Eucharist
Short Story: Maling's stomach mimics sounds, imitating air raid sirens at a company meeting
Author: wrote the screenplay for The Third Man; the famous 'cuckoo clock' line was not in his screenplay
Author: sued Yambo Ouologuem for plagiarizing It's a Battlefield
Author: helped R.K. Narayan get published; called him 'the Indian Chekhov'
Author: helped get Flann O'Brien's At Swim-Two-Birds published
Author: classified own novels into 'Catholic novels' and 'entertainments'
Author: Fox awarded damages after he claimed the studio was sexually exploiting Shirley Temple
Novel: middle-aged Henry Pulling becomes a smuggler in Paraguay through elderly Aunt Augusta; Brighton is his first destination
Author: converted to Catholicism in 1926 to marry dolls' house collector Vivien Dayrell-Browning
Author: played Russian roulette as a teen; Castro marveled at this when they met
Author: was himself an MI6 agent; stationed in Sierra Leone
Novel: Arthur Rowe given a cake containing secret film just before his house is destroyed during the Blitz
Novel: dancer Coral Musker and merchant Carleton Myatt begin an affair on the Orient Express
Novel: architect Querry moves to a Congolese leper colony
Novel: Dr. Eduardo Plarr dies; Charles Fortnum holds the title role
Author: commended Patrick Hamilton's The West Pier as 'the best book written about Brighton'
Author: friend of Panamanian dictator Omar Torrijos
